smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: 3 ads for Barrelhand
thread.body_text:
hey Karel, spent a couple minutes on the Barrelhand site and honestly can't figure out why we're not already working together haha.

anyway, that's not why I'm writing. I run Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of pitching you some BS, I actually built you a few. (gotta provide value before anything)

built 3 ad mockups from Barrelhand's real products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and reviews. it's done and sitting ready on my end.

want me to send it over?

- Kyle

Hi Kyle, 

 We are currently running meta ads, would be curious what you had in mind and how you would approach it differently.
 

 -Karel

here you go Karel - 3 ad mockups built from Barrelhand's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Barrelhand sells a $9,750 mechanical spaceflight watch called Monolith with no visible reviews and no ads we can find running, so this call is about building paid social from zero for a high-ticket, long-consideration product, not fixing a leaky account.

deep_dive.what_they_are: An engineering-first independent watch brand out of San Francisco. They frame themselves as a platform building tools for the next chapter of space exploration. They are not a heritage luxury house. They are a frontier-tech maker.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Their flagship is the Monolith, a mechanical toolwatch built for spaceflight, priced at $9,750. It comes in three strap colorways: Barrelhand Orange, Black, and Black & Orange. Next batch deliveries start Q4 2026 and orders are fully refundable before shipment. They also have a Memory Disc initiative and mention a Project One, but the Monolith is clearly the product that carries the brand.
deep_dive.positioning: Top-tier, technical, and mission-driven. They lean on real engineering language: understandable, serviceable, adaptable over time, built to endure harsh environments. This is a collector and enthusiast story, not a mass-market watch.
deep_dive.who_buys: Serious watch collectors and space-and-technology enthusiasts with high disposable income. People who want to be early to a frontier story and who value provable engineering over brand heritage.
strengths: A sharp, distinctive story. 'A window to the future' and 'spaceship for your wrist' are strong, ownable hooks that stand out in a feed.
A clear flagship product with a clean lineup of three colorways, which makes early ad testing simple. No sprawling catalog to sort through.
gaps: No reviews or social proof anywhere on the site. For a $9,750 purchase, trust is the whole game, and right now there is nothing on the page to build it.
No Meta ads we can find running, so there is no history of what creative or audience works. We are starting from a blank slate.

game_plan.headline: Build a cold-to-collector funnel from zero: lead with the Monolith's story to warm high-intent buyers, then use retargeting and trust-building to close a rare, high-ticket sale.

phases.goal: Prove that we can get the right people to stop, click, and raise their hand. At $9,750 we are not expecting sales in week one. We are proving demand and building an audience we can sell to.
campaigns: Cold prospecting to a small set of interest and lookalike audiences, sending traffic to the Monolith page and the newsletter for early access.
A basic retargeting campaign for anyone who visits the site or watches the ads, so no interest is wasted.
lead_products: Monolith is the only product we lead with. It is the flagship, it has a clear price and three colorways, and the whole brand story is built around it. There is no second product ready to carry ads yet.
phases.creative: Start with the three ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet. Lead with the strongest hook first: the 'spaceship for your wrist / window to the future' angle, because it is the most scroll-stopping and it captures the whole brand in one line. The other two ads run right behind it so we can see which story pulls hardest. All copy stays inside the playbook rules: no NASA claims, no 'flown in space,' no fake scarcity.
phases.budget: Start small and controlled while we learn.
phases.what_success_looks_like: We can see which ad and which audience pull the cheapest clicks and the most newsletter signups and add-to-carts. We have a growing retargeting pool. We are not judging this month on sales.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the winning ad and audience from month one, and widen the reach without losing quality. Start turning warm interest into first sales.
campaigns: Scale the winning cold ad and audience from Phase 1, and test two or three new lookalike audiences built off the people who engaged.

Build out proper retargeting: for people who viewed the Monolith page but did not act, show them the engineering proof, the serviceability story, and the fully-refundable-before-shipment offer to lower the risk of buying.
lead_products: Still Monolith. This is the month we start pushing the specific colorways, likely leading with Barrelhand Orange since it is the signature brand color and the most distinctive on screen.
phases.creative: Fresh versions of whatever won in month one. Add creative that answers the trust gap directly: close-up engineering detail, the 'serviceable and adaptable over time' promise, and the refundable-before-shipment safety net. This is where we work around the no-reviews problem.
phases.budget: Step up from the Phase 1 floor, weighted toward the winner.
phases.what_success_looks_like: First sales or serious sales conversations, a cost-per-click and cost-per-lead we trust, and a retargeting audience big enough to sell to steadily.

phases.goal: Scale the proven setup and introduce a second product to the funnel so we are not resting on one item.
campaigns: Scale the winning cold and retargeting campaigns with more budget, holding the cost-per-result steady.

Introduce a second product or initiative (the Memory Disc or Project One, depending on what is actually for sale) as a lower-friction entry point or a second story for the audience we have already built.
lead_products: Monolith stays the anchor. We add the Memory Disc or Project One as a second offer, but only after Karel confirms it is actually purchasable and at what price. We do not guess.
phases.creative: Best-performing Monolith ads at higher spend, plus new creative for the second product built in the same brand voice.
phases.budget: Highest of the three phases, only on what has proven it works.
phases.what_success_looks_like: A repeatable setup that produces sales at a cost Karel is happy with, plus a second product starting to pull its own weight.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.value: Around $100 to $150 a day to start
estimates.basis: Enough to get a few dozen clicks a day at typical costs in this category, which is the floor for learning which ad and audience work. For a $9,750 product we expect a long consideration window, so early spend is about learning, not sales.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We cannot put an honest number on this yet
estimates.basis: At $9,750 per unit, revenue depends almost entirely on how many buyers exist and how long they take to decide, and we have no view of their current traffic, list size, or past sales. We would need the numbers in the discovery section before promising any figure. Saying anything now would be a guess.

assumptions: The Monolith at $9,750 is the main product and the one worth advertising first.
There is enough margin in a $9,750 watch to fund paid ads even with a long sales cycle. If margin is thin because of build cost, the plan changes.
The site can take orders and the checkout works, or the newsletter/early-access path is the real conversion step for now.
There is no hidden library of reviews or testimonials we could be using. If there is, we build the whole plan around it much faster.

questions.question: You mentioned you're already running Meta ads. What are you spending a month right now, and who's running it?
questions.listening_for: Real current spend and whether it's in-house, a freelancer, or another agency. This tells Kyle if he's replacing someone or building fresh, and whether there's any account history to learn from.

questions.question: How's it been going so far? What have those ads actually done for you?
questions.listening_for: Sales, leads, or nothing. Whether Karel is happy or frustrated. If they've had zero sales from ads, that reframes the whole call around starting properly, not optimizing.

questions.question: On a $9,750 watch, roughly how much room is there after you cover the build cost? I'm not after exact numbers, just whether there's enough to fund ads.
questions.listening_for: Whether margin can carry paid ads for a long consideration product. A high-ticket item usually has room, but a hand-built watch can be expensive to make. If margin is thin, spend has to be tighter.

questions.question: How many Monoliths have you actually sold so far, and how long does a typical buyer take from first hearing about you to ordering?
questions.listening_for: The real demand and the length of the sales cycle. This is the single biggest thing shaping expectations. A months-long decision means month one is never about sales.

questions.question: When someone lands on the Monolith page today, what happens? Can they buy right there, or is it more about joining the list for the next batch?
questions.listening_for: Whether the conversion point is a direct purchase or a newsletter signup for Q4 2026 delivery. This decides what we even optimize the ads toward.

questions.question: You've got no reviews or customer stories on the site. Do you have any anywhere - emails, DMs, collector forum posts, press?
questions.listening_for: Any trust material at all. For a $9,750 unproven purchase this is the biggest gap. Even a few real owner quotes or a press mention changes what creative we can run.

questions.question: Which colorway sells best, and is there one you push hardest?
questions.listening_for: Whether Barrelhand Orange, Black, or Black & Orange leads. Helps decide which product image we lead with instead of guessing.

questions.question: The site mentions the Memory Disc and Project One. Are those actually for sale, and at what price?
questions.listening_for: Whether there's a real second product for Phase 3 and whether it's a cheaper entry point. If it's not buyable, we drop it from the plan.

questions.question: Who do you picture actually buying this - a watch collector, a space enthusiast, or someone else? Where do those people hang out?
questions.listening_for: How well Karel knows the buyer. Sharp answers help us build audiences fast. Vague answers mean month one is partly about finding the buyer.

questions.question: When people already know you exist, are they searching for 'Barrelhand' or 'Monolith watch' on Google? Do you see any search traffic?
questions.listening_for: Whether there's branded search demand worth catching with Google. For a high-ticket collector item, people who've heard the story often search the name later. If there's branded search, a small Google campaign catches ready-to-buy people cheaply. This is a real opening.

questions.question: Do you get any traffic from watch collector sites, forums, or reviewers writing about you?
questions.listening_for: Whether an SEO or content play makes sense. In the watch world, buyers research heavily before spending five figures. If people write about them, there's an SEO opening. If it's a brand-new category nobody searches, we'd hold off and say so.

objections.objection: We're already running Meta ads, why would we switch to you?
objections.answer: That's fair, and I'm not here to tell you what you've got is broken. I sent you three ads I built from your own product and a playbook of your voice before you'd given me anything, so you can already see how I think. What I'd want to know is what your current ads are actually returning. If they're working, great, I'll say so. If they're not, I've got a clear way to start from a proven setup instead of guessing.

objections.objection: Our watch is $9,750. Nobody buys something like that off a Facebook ad.
objections.answer: You're right that nobody buys a five-figure watch straight off one ad, and I'd never plan it that way. The ad's job is to get the right person to stop and raise their hand. Then we warm them up over weeks with the engineering story and your refundable-before-shipment offer, which takes the risk off them. Month one is about building that audience, not chasing an instant sale. I'd be lying if I promised sales in week one.

objections.objection: We don't have reviews or customer stories to show.
objections.answer: That's the one thing I flagged as the biggest gap, and it's honest of you to know it. On a purchase this size, trust does the closing. So early on we lean on what you do have: the engineering detail, the serviceability promise, and the fully-refundable-before-shipment safety net. And getting a few real owner quotes is one of the first things I'd want us to work on, because it makes everything else easier.

objections.objection: You don't know our numbers, so how can you promise this will work?
objections.answer: I won't pretend to know your numbers, because I can't see them, and any agency that quotes you a return before seeing your account is guessing. What I can promise is a careful start with small spend, so we prove what works before we put real money behind it. The questions I've got for you are exactly the ones I'd need answered to put honest figures on this.

red_flags.flag: No reviews or social proof anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: A $9,750 purchase lives or dies on trust. With nothing to reassure a cold buyer, ads will struggle to convert until we build some proof. This is the single biggest risk to the plan.

red_flags.flag: Very high price point with a long consideration window.
red_flags.why_it_matters: At $9,750, buyers take weeks or months to decide. Kyle must set the expectation up front that month one is about building an audience, not sales, or the client will judge it too early and churn.

red_flags.flag: Next batch deliveries not until Q4 2026 and orders refundable before shipment.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Buyers may hesitate to pay now for a watch that ships much later. It also means the real early conversion may be a newsletter or reservation signup, not a straight sale. We need to know what we're optimizing toward.

red_flags.flag: No Meta ads found running despite Karel saying they run them.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Either the ads are paused, tiny, or hard to find. Kyle should not assume there's a working account to build on. Confirm what's actually live before promising to improve it.

other_channel_openings: Google branded search: for a five-figure collector watch, people who hear the story often search the name later. A small Google campaign could catch those ready-to-buy people cheaply. Confirm branded search exists first.

SEO and content: watch buyers research heavily before spending big, and collector sites and forums drive real traffic. If reviewers write about Barrelhand, there's a content opening worth exploring. Only pursue if the discovery answers show real search interest.
